topic: Living vs Natural Earth vs Human-Made

bee_quiz.question: Anything connected to living things — alive, once alive, or made by living things — is called what?
Rocks, soil, sand, water, and air that exist in nature and were NOT made by humans or living things are called what?
A plastic bottle, wooden chair, and metal fork are all examples of what?
In the MRS GREN checklist, what does the first 'R' stand for?
Which of these is NOT part of the MRS GREN checklist for living things?
According to the 7-of-7 rule in science, something is alive if and only if it does which number of the MRS GREN processes?
A candle flame grows, eats fuel, moves, and releases smoke like waste. Is a flame alive?

choices.a: Yes, because it eats fuel and grows bigger
choices.b: Yes, because it moves and releases waste
choices.c: No, because it cannot reproduce and has no cells or DNA
choices.d: No, because flames are too hot to be living

bee_quiz.misconception_targeted: Flame shares some properties of life but lacks reproduction, DNA, and cells

bee_quiz.question: A wooden spoon is an example of what category?

choices.a: Biological, because it came from a tree
choices.b: Human-made, because a human deliberately shaped it
choices.c: Natural Earth, because wood comes from nature
choices.d: A mix of both biological and human-made

bee_quiz.misconception_targeted: When humans deliberately shape something, the product becomes human-made even if the raw material was biological

bee_quiz.question: A feather that fell off a bird yesterday is an example of what category?

choices.a: Natural Earth, because it was separated from the living bird
choices.c: Biological, because it grew from and is a product of a living thing
choices.d: Not in any category because it is not alive anymore

bee_quiz.misconception_targeted: Parts of living things remain biological even after they separate from the organism

bee_quiz.question: In the technology-building process, finding the raw material comes BEFORE which step?
After humans process raw materials and make individual parts, the next step in technology is what?
A bird builds a nest from twigs. Which of these is a step that the bird takes DIFFERENTLY than humans use technology?

choices.b: Uses instinct or learning from parents instead of written plans

bee_quiz.question: Which of these is NOT an example of something biological?

choices.b: A rock formed in the Earth
choices.c: A shell made by a mollusc

bee_quiz.question: All of these are clues scientists use to spot a living thing EXCEPT which one?

choices.a: Orientation — having a clear top and bottom
choices.d: Fine structure — detailed patterns under magnification

bee_quiz.question: All of these are involved in making technology EXCEPT which one?

choices.c: Living things reproducing to make offspring

word_cards.definition: Anything connected to living things — alive right now, once alive, part of a living thing, or made by a living thing.
word_cards.example: Sugar is biological because it comes from a sugarcane plant, even though the sugar granules don't look alive.

word_cards.definition: Things that exist in nature all by themselves and were not made by humans or living things.
word_cards.example: Rocks, soil, sand, water, and air are all examples of natural Earth materials.

word_cards.definition: Objects that humans deliberately shaped, processed, or built, usually starting from natural materials.
word_cards.example: A plastic bottle is human-made because humans used technology to process crude oil into plastic.

word_cards.definition: Having a clear top and bottom, a front and back — a definite right way to position something.
word_cards.example: A dog has a head end and a tail end, so it has orientation. A rock can be turned any way.

word_cards.definition: When two sides of something match each other perfectly, or when parts arrange in a matching circle pattern.
word_cards.example: Your left and right sides match — that is bilateral symmetry. A starfish has radial symmetry.

word_cards.definition: The process where living things use oxygen and food to release energy and create waste like carbon dioxide.
word_cards.example: When you breathe in oxygen and eat food, your body uses respiration to give you energy to move and think.

word_cards.definition: When living things create more of themselves — such as animals having babies or plants making seeds.
word_cards.example: A rabbit has baby rabbits. An apple tree grows seeds. Both are examples of reproduction.

word_cards.definition: Every step from finding raw materials to cutting, processing, assembling to create something humans designed.
word_cards.example: Making a tin of baked beans uses farming, canning, cooking, and shipping — all are parts of technology.

word_cards.definition: A checklist of 7 things scientists look for to decide if something is alive: Movement, Respiration, Sensitivity, Growth, Reproduction, Excretion, Nutrition.
word_cards.example: A plant does all 7 things on MRS GREN. A candle flame fails because it cannot reproduce or sense the environment.

word_cards.definition: When living things get rid of waste that their body doesn't need anymore.
word_cards.example: When you sweat, pee, breathe out carbon dioxide, or drop dead leaves, you are doing excretion.

word_cards.definition: Taking in food or energy from somewhere to help your body grow bigger and repair itself.
word_cards.example: You get nutrition when you eat food. A plant gets nutrition from soil, water, and sunlight.
